# <span id="page-3-0"></span>**1.0 Project Purpose and Description**

This study has been prepared by GM2 Associates, Inc. (GM2) to examine the potential traffic impacts associated with the proposed Chipotle Mexican Grill (Chipotle) restaurant development of the property located at 223 and 227 County Road in the Town of Barrington, Rhode Island.

The parcel is located on the east side of County Road, between Fairway Drive and Waseca Avenue/Markwood Drive. The site formerly included a gas station, current closed, and a small office building to the rear of the gas station, that building has also been removed. This study examines the off-site traffic impacts of the proposed site development.

The proposed development will consist of a new 2,345-square foot, 42-seat Chipotle restaurant as shown in **Figure 1**

The study components include the following:

#### Data Collection

A field review of existing physical conditions on County Road at the project site, Shopping Center and Rumstick Road was conducted and included road width, geometric alignment, lighting, signing, pavement markings, lane arrangements, traffic counts and operational characteristics. Crash data in the site vicinity was collected and reviewed. Traffic counts were taken at the site and at the intersection of County Road and Maple Avenue to the north during the weekday p.m. peak period and the Saturday peak period. Counts were performed by Precision Data Industries, LLC (PDI).

#### Traffic Projections

Trip generation for the proposed development were estimated, and the projected trips were distributed to the surrounding street network. These estimated volumes were added to five-year projections of the existing volumes, with an annual rate increase of 0.5% per year, for the analysis of the projected conditions.

#### Analysis

Capacity analyses for projected traffic volumes were conducted at the site driveway and at the nearby major intersections as a reference for traffic operational impacts. The analyses were conducted for the "No-Build" and "Build" conditions. Safety analyses were conducted and included the evaluation of sight distances at the proposed driveway and the review of available crash data.

#### Conclusions and Recommendations

GM2 prepared the findings and recommendations based on our study of the project area.

![](1. Traffic Impact Study_attachments/_page_4_Figure_0.jpeg)

### <span id="page-5-0"></span>**2.0 Existing Conditions**

The project site is located at 223 and 227 County Road in the Town of Barrington, Rhode Island, as shown in **Figure 2**, attached on the following page.

The proposed Chipotle Mexican Grill (Chipotle or restaurant) will include site access via the unsignalized driveway at County Road. The site plan is referenced as **Figure 1***,* as noted in Section 1.0.

### <span id="page-5-1"></span>**2.1 Site and Roadway Conditions**

GM2 has identified the following streets and intersections as potential areas of impact for the proposed restaurant:

County Road at the project site is a three-lane roadway, 42 feet in width with no on-street parking. The roadway cross section includes one 11- foot travel lane in each direction separated by a 12 foot two-way-left-turn lane in the center and 4-foot curb offsets on each side. There is 8 feet of sidewalk area on the east side of the roadway; face of curb to the state highway line. County Road (RI Routes 103 and 114) runs in a north-south direction and is classified as a principal arterial highway according to Technical Paper No. 155, Highway Functional Classification System for the State of Rhode Island, 2005-2015, published by the Rhode Island Statewide Planning Program. At the project site, County Road is on a tangent alignment, with a roadway grade of approximately 0.6 percent descending from north to south. North of the site, County Road curves slightly to the east, and south of the site, it curves to the west. There is curbing and a sidewalk on both sides of the street. The bituminous pavement in the through travel lanes is in fair-to-good condition; the center two-way-left-turn lane is formed of colored concrete. Street lighting, in the form of period lighting, is provided throughout this section of County Road. The section of County Road through the center of the town has a speed limit of 25 miles per hour.

The project site presently has two, two-way driveways on County Road. The northerly driveway is 30 feet wide. The southerly driveway is 37 feet wide. The site plan for the proposed Chipotle will consolidate the driveways to one curb cut on the site at the north end of the parcel. The southerly driveway will be eliminated. The new driveway will be 24 feet wide.

Nearby signalized intersections include:

- County Road at Rumstick Road
- East Bay Bike Path
- County Road at Church Driveway/ Barrington Shopping Center Drive
- County Road at Maple Avenue/Town Hall

# <span id="page-7-0"></span>**2.2 Traffic Volumes**

Traffic data were collected by PDI, LLC in the form of manual turning movement counts at three intersections in the project vicinity:

- County Road at Rumstick Road
- County Road at Church Driveway/ Barrington Shopping Center Drive
- County Road at Maple Avenue/Town Hall

All count data are included in the appendices. Note that data for County Road may be coded as east-west, but all data used was properly oriented for input into analyses.

Counts were completed between 4:00 p.m. and 6:00 p.m. on Thursday January 5, 2023 and between 12:00 p.m. and 2:00 p.m. on Saturday, January 7, 2023. These periods were chosen to establish the peak traffic conditions County Road at the site as well as the projected peak time periods of the Chipotle restaurant.

For the purposes of this study, the peak periods are defined as follows:

- 4:15 p.m. to 5:15 p.m. during weekday (Thursday) (note consistent band in 2-hour period)
- 12:00 p.m. to 1:00 p.m. during Saturday

The existing peak hour volumes are shown in **Figures 3 and 4**.

The traffic counts were expanded using factors developed by the Rhode Island Department of Transportation to estimate the average daily traffic volumes along County Road. The factors used are developed by RIDOT based on empirical data collected throughout the state and represent a percentage of daily traffic that can be expected during any given hour of the day, day of the week, and month of the year. Note that the accuracy of the expanded data is likely low due to the limited number of hours used to make the calculations.

AADTs (vehicles per day) on County Road were computed as follows:

- South of Rumstick Road 17,100 (Thu)/22,500 (Sat)
- South of Church Driveway/ Barrington Shopping Center Drive 17,700 (Thu)/23,100 (Sat)
- South of Maple Avenue/Town Hall 16,750 (Thu)/22,300 (Sat)

These volumes are comparable to the weekday volumes counted in this area in 2018 under a previous traffic impact study for the same site. Under those collected volumes, at the project site, County Road carries approximately 19,100 vehicles per day. At Maple Avenue, County Road carries approximately 17,300 vpd on the southerly leg of the intersection and approximately 14,600 vpd on the north side of the intersection.

# <span id="page-10-0"></span>**2.3 Existing Traffic Operations and Level of Service**

The accepted practice for traffic analysis for this type of study involves completing a review of the existing conditions, the five-year no-build conditions, and the five-year build conditions. The results of these analyses are used to evaluate the projected impacts of the proposed development and to determine if any of those impacts are significant enough to warrant mitigating treatments to existing and/or proposed intersections, roadways, or driveways. These analyses were completed using the Synchro software package (11th Edition) which relies on the Highway Capacity Manual, which is the standard used in the profession. At the time of writing the Synchro version used is the most current available.

The results of a capacity analysis are expressed in terms of "Level of Service" (LOS). LOS is a measure of driver comfort and convenience under a given set of roadway and traffic conditions. LOS is ranked from "A" through "F," with "A" representing free flow conditions, "C" representing average delay, and "F" representing conditions that include long delays to motorists. Note that the measures are different for signalized versus unsignalized intersections as shown below:

<span id="page-10-1"></span>**LOS Delay (Seconds) LOS Delay (Seconds)** A 10 A ≤10 B >10 & ≤20 B >10 & ≤15 C >20 & ≤35 C >15 & ≤25 D >35 & ≤55 D >25 & ≤35 E >55 & ≤80 E >35 & ≤50 F >80 F >50 **Signalized Intersections Unsignalized Intersections**

**Table 1 - Level of Service Criteria**

GM2 reviewed the intersections listed below and established the existing conditions analyses, the results of which are shown in **Table 2**. As indicated, the analyses show that the three observed intersections are operating well during the observed peak hours.

The analyses for future conditions are provided later in this report.

Intersections analyzed:

- County Road at Maple Avenue/Town Hall
- County Road at Church Driveway/ Barrington Shopping Center Drive
- County Road at Rumstick Road

# <span id="page-12-0"></span>**2.4 Safety Analysis**

A safety analysis is required to determine if there are any concerning deficiencies in the project study area that might be exacerbated by the proposed development. The review consists of reviewing crash data in the immediate area and identifying any patterns that may or may not be occurring and using engineering judgement to determine if the proposed development might contribute to these and determine if mitigation is needed. In addition, a review of the sight distances into or out of the proposed development is done to determine if there are any potential issues as a result of vehicles entering or exiting the roadway at the location.

# <span id="page-12-1"></span>**2.4.1 Crash Analysis**

The crash history for County Road in the project vicinity was investigated through the Barrington Police Department. Crash data for all of 2015-2017, and for January 1 through May 9, 2018, a period of over three years and four months, was reviewed. In that entire period, only one crash was recorded at the project site (in 2016, with no injuries). As described in Section 2.1 of this report, the project site is located on a straight section of County Road, where the vertical grade is very slight at 0.6%, meaning the road is essentially flat. Neither the accident history nor the physical conditions indicate any traffic safety concerns at the proposed Chipotle location.

### <span id="page-12-2"></span>**2.4.2 Sight Distance Analysis**

The locations of the proposed driveway was evaluated for sight distance. The analysis is based on criteria published by the American Association of State Highway and Transportation Officials (AASHTO) in A Policy on Geometric Design of Highways and Streets - 2018.

The available sight distance from the proposed driveway was observed in the field and measured using Google Maps.

As previously mentioned, the speed limit on this section of County Road is 25 MPH. The safe stopping sight distance for 25 mph is 155 feet. The field observations made were from the AASHTO-recommended position of 14.5 feet from the edge of the travel lane and from a more comfortable driver stopping location which places the driver's eye at approximately the back of the sidewalk, or 11.5 feet from the edge of the travel lane. The available sight distances are about the same from either position and are as follows:

From proposed driveway

Looking north approximately 425 feet Looking south approximately 725 feet

As shown, the available sight distances from the proposed driveway at the project site exceed that required for the speed limit. (For informational purposes, the above sight distances exceed that of 305 feet required for a higher travel speed of 40 mph.)

![](1. Traffic Impact Study_attachments/_page_12_Picture_13.jpeg)

### <span id="page-13-0"></span>**3.0 Projected Conditions**

The proposed project includes a single, free-standing building that will house a new Chipotle Mexican Grill Restaurant. The proposed building will be approximately 2,400 square feet in size and is planned as a dine-in/take-out only location with no drive-thru. One curb opening on County Road is planned at the north end of the site.

# <span id="page-13-1"></span>**3.1 No-Build Conditions**

The No-Build condition serves to address normal traffic growth as well as to account for any other developments in the project vicinity that are expected to be constructed and operational in the near future. This period is typically five years from the expected date of completion of the project being studied and is the universally accepted standard for this type of effort. GM2 used a growth rate of 0.5 percent per year, which is the generally accepted growth rate at the statewide level for developed urban areas such as Barrington. The No-Build Condition year is thus established as 2028, and the projected traffic volumes are shown in **Figures 5 and 6**.

### <span id="page-13-2"></span>**3.1.1 Projected Background Traffic**

In order to properly assess the potential impacts of the additional traffic created from the project development, the future background growth mentioned above and other developments must also be considered.

GM2 has not been made aware of any other project developments in the area, and therefore no additional traffic is required to be added to the No-Build Condition beyond the 0.5% growth rate.

# <span id="page-16-0"></span>**3.2 Chipotle Restaurant**

### <span id="page-16-1"></span>**3.2.1 Trip Generation**

The usual practice for trip generation estimates for proposed developments is based on the data published by the Institute of Transportation Engineers (ITE) in its Trip Generation publication. ITE is a national organization of traffic and transportation engineers, and ITE's trip generation data are the most thorough and authoritative in the profession. The publications used are typically the most current available (11th Edition). Under certain circumstances, there is an opportunity to use data from other similar projects. GM2 received data from a study completed in Medfield, Massachusetts for a Chipotle of the same configuration. In that study, the Engineer used the ITE rates and no "built location data" are yet available.

GM2 has reviewed the applicable Land Use codes and has determined that the code used in the Medfield study also applies to this Barrington study. Land Use Code 930 – Fast Casual Restaurant, was used as it applies to a casual sit-down restaurant, where patrons order from a menu board at a counter, pay, and seat themselves with no wait staff or table service. This land use code is separate from others that include a drive-thru. This study site and the Medfield site do not include a drive-thru.

GM2 reviewed the Data Plots and Equations for each condition and determined the following:

- In the p.m. peak, the Average Rate should be used, not the Fitted Curve Equation, based on the range of similar data points. Use the Weekday, Peak Hour of Adjacent Street Traffic, One Hour between 4 and 6 p.m.
- In the Saturday peak, the Average Rate should be used, as no Fitted Curve Equation exists due to too few data points. Use Saturday, Peak Hour of Generator condition.

<span id="page-16-2"></span>The results are shown in the following table:

**Table 3 - Trip Generation – Fast Casual Restaurant**

|                       |                                                                      | Land Use Code 930 - Fast Casual Restaurant |         |    |    |          |    |  |  |  |  |
|-----------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------|---------|----|----|----------|----|--|--|--|--|
| Assumptions:          |                                                                      |                                            | PM PEAK |    |    | SAT PEAK |    |  |  |  |  |
| Area                  | 2,400 SF (X=2.4)<br>Enter<br>Exit<br>TOTAL<br>Enter<br>Exit<br>TOTAL |                                            |         |    |    |          |    |  |  |  |  |
| Avg                   |                                                                      | 17                                         | 14      | 31 | 49 | 29       | 78 |  |  |  |  |
| Fitted Curve Equation |                                                                      | 15                                         | 12      | 27 | -  | -        | -  |  |  |  |  |

Once the trip generation has been calculated, it must be determined where the trips are coming from and going to before they can be assigned to the network. This step is required to determine if existing traffic will use the site, if new trips will be made from other regions, or trips within a local network that will re-route to go to the restaurant. These are estimated as follows:

Diverted trips. The come from traffic on nearby roads attracted to the site without direct access to the site. In this particular location, there are no other Chipotle Restaurants nearby and County Road is a principal arterial with no other similar roads nearby. In essence, there are no trips being removed from one roadway and added to County Road as an interim stop while on the way to a primary destination on the road they were originally on, or the percentage is so low, that it is negligible.

![](1. Traffic Impact Study_attachments/_page_16_Picture_14.jpeg)

Pass-by trips. The pass-by traffic, i.e., traffic that is already on the street, makes an intermediate stop along the way and then continues on its original path, has been estimated at 50% based on the ITE Trip Generation Pass-By Tables for the nearest applicable Land Use Code, Code 932. These tables provide data that is not current, thus assumptions to the applicability must be made. This 50% value can be considered conservative, meaning that a large portion of the trips assumed to be associated with the Chipotle Restaurant will be designated as new trips.

New trips. The balance of the site trip generation is assumed to be newly-generated trips. After accounting for the above two traffic components of diverted trips and pass-by trips, the newlygenerated trips at the proposed restaurant are estimated to constitute the remaining 50 percent of the total trips entering and exiting.

<span id="page-17-1"></span>The following table summarizes the pass-by and new trips:

**TOTAL Pass-by New TOTAL Pass-by New Enter** 17 8 9 **Enter** 49 24 25 **Exit** 14 7 7 **Exit** 29 14 15 **TOTAL** 31 15 16 **TOTAL** 78 38 40 **PM PEAK Saturday PEAK**

**Table 4 - Trip Generation Composition**

Note that once the trips are distributed, the actual percentages vary and are closer to 48% / 52%.

# <span id="page-17-0"></span>**3.2.3 Trip Distribution and Traffic Assignment**

Once a summary of the trips has been determined, those trips are then assigned travel paths through the roadway system. Note that in either peak period, the total number of new trips is very low.

GM2 then examined the local roadway network and neighborhoods and made assumptions on the distribution of traffic accordingly. Those volumes were distributed as shown in **Figures 7 and 8.**

**The values shown in these figures include pass-by (subtractions from the main movements) and new trips (additions to the main movements). The format of Figures 7 and 8 includes both. Note the legend on the Figures.** 

The site-generated traffic was then assigned to the 2028 No-Build volumes using the trip generation estimates and trip distribution described above to determine the 2028 Build condition volumes as shown in **Figures 9 and 10**.

# <span id="page-22-0"></span>**3.3 Capacity Analysis**

The projected conditions for this study were developed for a five-year time horizon as previously discussed. To develop the projected peak hourly volumes, the background traffic volumes counted were estimated to increase at the rate of 0.5 percent per year. This rate was applied to the existing traffic and resulted in the traffic volumes shown previously in **Figures 5 and 6**, the 2028 No-Build Conditions. The results of these analyses are shown in **Table 5**.

Using the Synchro capacity analysis software, the trips were put onto the built roadway system and results were generated for the Build Condition, for each peak period, for each intersection, as shown in **Figures 9 and 10**. The results of these analyses are also shown in **Table 5**, alongside the No-Build results.

**Table 5 - 2028 Capacity Analyses Summary**

| County Road Southbound Thru/Right     | C/21.0  | C/25.3                        | C/21.2  | C/26.1                        |
| County Road Northbound Left           | B/12.5  | A/9.6                         | B/12.8  | A/9.8                         |
| County Road Northbound Thru/Right     | A/7.3   | A/8.4                         | A/7.4   | A/8.4                         |
| Intersection                          | B/13.9  | B/15.9                        | B/14.0  | B/16.3                        |
| County Road at Shopping Plaza/Church  | PM Peak | SAT Peak                      | PM Peak | SAT Peak                      |

| Shopping Plaza Right                  | A/5.6   | A/5.5                         | A/5.6   | A/5.5                         |

![](1. Traffic Impact Study_attachments/_page_22_Picture_7.jpeg)

As expected, the no-build conditions analyses are nearly identical to the existing conditions as the variance is essentially a daily change and not significant.

As noted previously, the total number of new trips generated by the Chipotle Restaurant is expected to be insignificant to the total volume of existing traffic. As such, the number of trips is not as critical as the creation of an unsignalized driveway. GM2 notes that the site under consideration in this study was previously a gas station (6 pump) with a service garage, which generally generates more trips than a fast-food restaurant in the peak hour of the generator. The site also previously had two driveways and these are intended to be consolidated into one under the new build condition.

The site driveway is expected to have reasonable operations as the two-way-left-turn-lane (TWLTL) provides an area for southbound left-turns to exit the southbound traffic flow and for leftturns out of the site it provides a refuge area so that a two-stage crossing can be made.

The sprawling roadway network serves to readily absorb the additional traffic, and operations are expected to remain essentially unchanged from the present conditions.

The trips distributed to the other study intersections are also negligible and have insignificant changes to the signal operations.

# <span id="page-23-0"></span>**3.4 County Road Summer Operations**

GM2 recognizes that these counts were taken during the winter season and do not reflect the impacts of the signal at the East Bay Bike Path on overall operations along County Road.

Historical count data was obtained from the RIDOT for the location north of Massasoit Drive and while these volumes do not directly correlate to this site, they do indicate an overall pattern that can be expected during the summer months.

There is an understanding that congestion is prevalent at times, with notable periods on Saturdays when the bike path traffic is heavy. Congestion and back-up on County Road result not from additional volume, but a decrease in capacity of the roadway caused by the bike path signal stopping traffic.

![](1. Traffic Impact Study_attachments/_page_23_Picture_11.jpeg)

# <span id="page-24-0"></span>**4.0 Summary and Conclusion**

The study was performed using conservative traffic volumes, trip generation values, and the operations at the intersections were calculated using conservative parameters.

The safety analysis did not reveal any existing recurring patterns that would be notably increased with the additional traffic expected as a result of the proposed development.

Based on the analysis conducted for this study, the proposed development of the Chipotle Mexican Grill restaurant is expected to have minimal impacts on the surrounding roadway network during both of the studied peak periods.

+ + +

# **Appendix C**

# **TRIP GENERATION CHARTS**

![](1. Traffic Impact Study_attachments/_page_129_Picture_4.jpeg)

# Land Use: 930 Fast Casual Restaurant

# **Description**

A fast casual restaurant is a sit-down restaurant with no (or very limited) wait staff or table service. A customer typically orders off a menu board, pays for food before the food is prepared, and seats themselves. The menu generally contains higher-quality, made-to-order food items with fewer frozen or processed ingredients than at a fast-food restaurant. Most patrons eat their meal within the restaurant, but a significant proportion of the restaurant sales can be carry-out orders. A fast casual restaurant typically serves lunch and dinner; some serve breakfast. A typical duration of stay for an eat-in customer is 40 minutes or less. Fine dining restaurant (Land Use 931), high-turnover (sit-down) restaurant (Land Use 932), and fast-food restaurant without drivethrough window (Land Use 933) are related uses.

# **Additional Data**

The fast casual restaurant study sites included in this land use did not have a drive-through window.

The technical appendices provide supporting information on time-of-day distributions for this land use. The appendices can be accessed through either the ITETripGen web app or the trip generation resource page on the ITE website (https://www.ite.org/technical-resources/topics/tripand-parking-generation/).

The sites were surveyed in the 2010s in Minnesota, South Carolina, Washington, and Wisconsin.
